card.intuition: dp[i][j] = True if s[i..j] is a palindrome. Base: all single chars are palindromes (dp[i][i]=True), adjacent equal chars (dp[i][i+1] = s[i]==s[i+1]). Expansion: dp[i][j] = (s[i]==s[j]) and dp[i+1][j-1]. Fill by increasing substring length.
card.analogy: Checking if a nested set of parentheses is balanced: once you confirm the inner brackets are balanced, you just check if the outer pair matches. Palindromes work the same way — check borders, then trust the inner result.
card.time_complexity: O(n²) for the DP table; Manacher's is O(n) for all palindromic substrings
card.space_complexity: O(n²) for dp table; O(n) for Manacher's
when_to_use: Minimum cuts to partition a string into palindromes
gotchas: Fill the table by LENGTH of substring (len=1, then len=2, ...) not by row — dp[i+1][j-1] must be filled before dp[i][j]
Longest palindromic SUBSEQUENCE ≠ substring — subsequence uses LCS(s, reverse(s))
For minimum palindrome partition, use a second DP on top of the palindrome table

exercise.prompt: Find the longest palindromic substring using DP.
hints: s[i..j] is a palindrome if s[i]==s[j] AND the inner substring s[i+1..j-1] is also a palindrome — check dp[i+1][j-1]
exercise.test_code:
result = longest_palindrome('babad')
assert result in ('bab', 'aba'), f'Got {result}'
assert longest_palindrome('cbbd') == 'bb'
assert longest_palindrome('a') == 'a'
assert longest_palindrome('ac') in ('a','c')
assert longest_palindrome('racecar') == 'racecar'
print('PASS')
